input.background: You have the capital to invest in two of four ventures, each of which requires approximately the same amount of investment capital. Unknown to you, two of the investments will eventually fail and two will be successful. You research the four ventures because you think that your research will increase your probability of a successful choice over a purely random selection, and you eventually decide on two.
input.question: If you used none of the information generated by your research and selected two ventures at random, what is the probability that you would select at least one successful venture?

output.answer:
Step 1: Denote the two successful enterprises as $S_1$ and $S_2$ and the two failing enterprises as $F_1$ and $F_2$. The experiment involves a random selection of two out of the four ventures, and each possible pair of ventures represents a sample point.
Step 2: The six sample points that make up the sample space are
1. $\left(S_1, S_2\right)$
2. $\left(S_1, F_1\right)$
3. $\left(S_1, F_2\right)$
4. $\left(S_2, F_1\right)$
5. $\left(S_2, F_2\right)$
6. $\left(F_1, F_2\right)$

Step 3: Next, we assign probabilities to the sample points. If we assume that the choice of any one pair is as likely as any other, then the probability of each sample point is $\frac{1}{6}$,
Step 4: The event of selecting at least one of the two successful ventures includes all the sample points except $\left(F_1, F_2\right)$.
Step 5: Now, we find

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text { Select at least one success })= & P\left(S_1, S_2\right)+P\left(S_1, F_1\right)+P\left(S_1, F_2\right)+ \\
& P\left(S_2, F_1\right)+P\left(S_2, F_2\right) \\
= & \frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{5}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, with a random selection, the probability of selecting at least one successful venture out of two is $\frac{5}{6}$.

meta_info.caption: Another Compound Event Probability-Investing in a Successful Venture
